ReproduceYostIndex Walkthrough

Author

Rafael Catoia Pulgrossi | QSU | Tae Yoon Lee | Summer Han

Published

April 14, 2026

Two Ways to Get Yost Index Data

ReproduceYostIndex provides two functions that serve different needs:

getYostIndex() computeYostIndex()
Census API key Not required Required
Speed Instant (pre-computed) Minutes to hours
Years available 2013–2023 2011–present
Geographies County, tract, block group County, tract, block group, state
Scope National, state National, state, county
Variants returned All four at once One (your chosen parameters)
Best for Most analyses, quick exploration Custom pipelines, newest ACS years

Start with getYostIndex(). It downloads pre-computed files from GitHub — no Census API key, no wait, all four Yost variants in a single call. Switch to computeYostIndex() only when you need a year not yet pre-computed, a county-level scope, or a fully custom pipeline.

getYostIndex() — Recommended Starting Point

Installation

# install.packages("devtools")
devtools::install_github("HanLabCollaboration/ReproduceYostIndex")

Basic usage

Code 
library(ReproduceYostIndex)

# All US counties, 2022 — no Census API key needed
county_yost <- getYostIndex(geo = "county", year = 2022)
head(county_yost)
# A tibble: 6 × 12
  GEOID  year geo    scope     Yost YostQuintile YostStabilized
  <chr> <dbl> <chr>  <chr>    <dbl>        <dbl>          <dbl>
1 01001  2022 county national 10.7             4          10.7 
2 01003  2022 county national 11.0             5          11.0 
3 01005  2022 county national  8.36            1           8.33
4 01007  2022 county national  8.88            1           8.91
5 01009  2022 county national  9.57            2           9.51
6 01011  2022 county national  8.25            1           8.27
# ℹ 5 more variables: YostStabilizedQuintile <dbl>, YostImputed <dbl>,
#   YostImputedQuintile <dbl>, YostStabilizedImputed <dbl>,
#   YostStabilizedImputedQuintile <dbl>

A single call returns all four Yost variants side by side:

Column Description
Yost / YostQuintile Baseline — no stabilization, no imputation
YostStabilized / YostStabilizedQuintile Empirical Bayes stabilization applied
YostImputed / YostImputedQuintile Spatial imputation for missing values
YostStabilizedImputed / YostStabilizedImputedQuintile Both stabilization and imputation

Filtering by state and scope

Code 
# Tracts for California and New York, ranked within each state
tracts_ca_ny <- getYostIndex(
  geo    = "tract",
  year   = 2022,
  states = c("CA", "NY"),
  scope  = "state"
)
glimpse(tracts_ca_ny)
Rows: 14,540
Columns: 12
$ GEOID                         <chr> "06001400100", "06001400200", "060014003…
$ year                          <dbl> 2022, 2022, 2022, 2022, 2022, 2022, 2022…
$ geo                           <chr> "tract", "tract", "tract", "tract", "tra…
$ scope                         <chr> "state", "state", "state", "state", "sta…
$ Yost                          <dbl> 11.719940, 11.565475, 11.422373, 11.3981…
$ YostQuintile                  <dbl> 5, 5, 5, 5, 4, 5, 4, 4, 4, 4, 4, 5, 4, 3…
$ YostStabilized                <dbl> 11.723930, 11.579934, 11.440019, 11.4030…
$ YostStabilizedQuintile        <dbl> 5, 5, 5, 5, 4, 5, 4, 4, 4, 5, 4, 5, 4, 3…
$ YostImputed                   <dbl> 11.721709, 11.563503, 11.418453, 11.3952…
$ YostImputedQuintile           <dbl> 5, 5, 5, 5, 4, 5, 4, 4, 4, 4, 4, 5, 4, 3…
$ YostStabilizedImputed         <dbl> 11.729109, 11.583589, 11.444651, 11.4110…
$ YostStabilizedImputedQuintile <dbl> 5, 5, 5, 5, 4, 5, 4, 4, 4, 5, 4, 5, 4, 3…

Choosing a scope

  • "national" (default): quintiles rank all US geographies against each other. Use when comparing across states.
  • "state": quintiles rank geographies within each state separately. Use when within-state comparisons matter.
Code 
# National scope — California counties ranked against all US counties
ca_national <- getYostIndex(geo = "county", year = 2022,
                            states = "CA", scope = "national")

# State scope — California counties ranked among themselves only
ca_state    <- getYostIndex(geo = "county", year = 2022,
                            states = "CA", scope = "state")

# Compare quintile distributions
table(national = ca_national$YostQuintile,
      state    = ca_state$YostQuintile)
        state
national  1  2  3  4  5
       2  5  0  0  0  0
       3  7  3  0  0  0
       4  0  9  4  0  0
       5  0  0  7 11 11

Choosing a Yost variant

Pick the column that fits your analysis:

Code 
df <- getYostIndex(geo = "county", year = 2022, states = "CA")

# For most county/tract analyses: plain Yost
hist(df$Yost, main = "Yost (baseline)", xlab = "Yost score", col = "steelblue", border = "white")
Code 
# For block groups or small-sample tracts: stabilized variant
# df$YostStabilized  — shrinks unreliable estimates toward their parent geography

# When there are many NAs: imputed variant
# df$YostImputed     — fills missing values via spatial mean of neighbors

Caching

Files are downloaded once and cached locally. Subsequent calls with the same arguments return instantly:

Code 
# First call: downloads ~2 MB file
df <- getYostIndex(geo = "county", year = 2022)

# Second call: reads from cache — instantaneous
df <- getYostIndex(geo = "county", year = 2022)

# Force a fresh download
df <- getYostIndex(geo = "county", year = 2022, cache = FALSE)

# See where the cache lives
tools::R_user_dir("ReproduceYostIndex", "cache")

computeYostIndex() — Full Pipeline

Use computeYostIndex() when:

  • You need a year not yet in the pre-computed files (e.g., 2024 ACS once released)
  • You need county-level scope (quintiles within each county)
  • You want to inspect intermediate outputs (raw ACS values, factor loadings, imputed data)
  • You are building a custom pipeline and need fine-grained control

Census API key (required)

# Get a free key at: https://api.census.gov/data/key_signup.html
tidycensus::census_api_key("YOUR_KEY_HERE", install = TRUE)

Basic usage

Code 
yost_ca <- computeYostIndex(
  geo       = "tract",
  year      = 2022,
  states    = "CA",
  scope     = "state",
  stabilize = TRUE,    # empirical Bayes stabilization
  impute    = TRUE,    # spatial imputation for missing values
  keep_geometry = TRUE
)
Code 
# Baseline (always present — no stabilization, no imputation)
head(yost_ca$df_yost_raw)
# A tibble: 6 × 4
  GEOID        Yost YostQuintile annotation   
  <chr>       <dbl> <fct>        <chr>        
1 06001400100  11.7 5            Complete data
2 06001400200  11.6 5            Complete data
3 06001400300  11.4 5            Complete data
4 06001400400  11.4 5            Complete data
5 06001400500  10.7 4            Complete data
6 06001400600  11.2 5            Complete data
Code 
# Requested variant (stabilize = TRUE, impute = TRUE)
head(yost_ca$df_yost)
# A tibble: 6 × 4
  GEOID       YostStabilizedImputed YostStabilizedImputedQuintile annotation   
  <chr>                       <dbl> <fct>                         <chr>        
1 06001400100                  11.7 5                             Complete data
2 06001400200                  11.6 5                             Complete data
3 06001400300                  11.4 5                             Complete data
4 06001400400                  11.4 5                             Complete data
5 06001400500                  10.7 4                             Complete data
6 06001400600                  11.3 5                             Complete data

Understanding the output list

Code 
str(yost_ca, max.level = 1)
List of 7
 $ df_yost_raw  : tibble [9,129 × 4] (S3: tbl_df/tbl/data.frame)
 $ df_yost      : tibble [9,129 × 4] (S3: tbl_df/tbl/data.frame)
 $ df_raw_values: tibble [9,129 × 71] (S3: tbl_df/tbl/data.frame)
 $ df_geometry  : sf [9,129 × 2] (S3: sf/tbl_df/tbl/data.frame)
  ..- attr(*, "sf_column")= chr "geometry"
  ..- attr(*, "agr")= Factor w/ 3 levels "constant","aggregate",..: NA
  .. ..- attr(*, "names")= chr "GEOID"
 $ df_imputed   : tibble [9,080 × 11] (S3: tbl_df/tbl/data.frame)
 $ df_rank      : tibble [9,074 × 8] (S3: tbl_df/tbl/data.frame)
 $ obj_factor   :List of 1
Element Contents
df_yost_raw Baseline Yost — always Yost + YostQuintile, regardless of parameters
df_yost Requested variant — column name reflects what was applied
df_raw_values Raw ACS estimates and margins of error
df_geometry Spatial geometries (if keep_geometry = TRUE)
df_imputed Component variables after imputation
df_rank Ranked/standardized inputs to factor analysis
obj_factor psych::fa object(s)

Stabilization explained

At fine geographic levels (especially block groups), ACS margins of error can exceed the estimates themselves. The empirical Bayes stabilization method pulls each lower-level estimate toward its parent geography using a data-adaptive weight:

\[w = \frac{t^2}{s^2 + t^2}\]

  • \(t^2\): within-parent heterogeneity — how much the units in this parent actually differ
  • \(s^2 = (\text{MOE}/1.645)^2\): sampling variance from the ACS margin of error

When a unit is reliable (\(s^2\) small), \(w \approx 1\) — the estimate is used as-is. When a unit is noisy (\(s^2\) large), \(w \approx 0\) — the estimate is pulled toward the parent.

Code 
# Compare raw vs stabilized
comparison <- yost_ca$df_yost_raw |>
  dplyr::rename(Yost_raw = Yost) |>
  dplyr::left_join(
    yost_ca$df_yost |>
      dplyr::rename(Yost_stabilized = YostStabilizedImputed),
    by = c("GEOID", "annotation")
  )

ggplot(comparison, aes(x = Yost_raw, y = Yost_stabilized)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.15, size = 0.6, color = "steelblue") +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, color = "red",
              linetype = "dashed", linewidth = 0.8) +
  labs(
    title    = "Raw vs. Stabilized Yost Index (CA tracts, 2022)",
    subtitle = "Points off the diagonal were pulled toward their parent geography",
    x        = "Yost (raw, no stabilization)",
    y        = "YostStabilizedImputed",
    caption  = "Red line = no change"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(plot.title    = element_text(face = "bold", hjust = 0.5),
        plot.subtitle = element_text(hjust = 0.5, color = "grey40"))
Warning: Removed 554 rows containing missing values or values outside the scale range
(`geom_point()`).

Mapping with computeYostIndex()

Because computeYostIndex() can return geometry directly, mapping is straightforward:

Code 
df_map <- yost_ca$df_geometry |>
  dplyr::left_join(yost_ca$df_yost, by = "GEOID")

ggplot(df_map) +
  geom_sf(aes(fill = YostStabilizedImputed), color = "white", linewidth = 0.02) +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(option = "plasma", name = "Yost\n(Stabilized)") +
  labs(
    title    = "Neighborhood SES — California Census Tracts",
    subtitle = "YostStabilizedImputed | 2022 ACS | State scope",
    caption  = "Higher values = higher socioeconomic status"
  ) +
  theme_void() +
  theme(
    plot.title    = element_text(face = "bold", size = 15, hjust = 0.5),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 11, hjust = 0.5, color = "grey40"),
    plot.caption  = element_text(hjust = 0.5, color = "grey60")
  )

When to use scope = "county"

County-level scope is only available in computeYostIndex(). It ranks geographies within each county, which is useful for within-county resource allocation or studies confined to a single metro area:

Code 
# Tracts in Los Angeles County ranked among themselves
yost_la <- computeYostIndex(
  geo    = "tract",
  year   = 2022,
  states = "CA",
  scope  = "county"   # not available in getYostIndex()
)

Decision Guide

Question If YES → If NO →
Do I have a Census API key? Either function works Use getYostIndex()
Do I need data for 2024 or later? Use computeYostIndex() Use getYostIndex()
Do I need county-level scope? Use computeYostIndex() Use getYostIndex()
Do I need intermediate outputs (raw ACS, factor loadings)? Use computeYostIndex() Use getYostIndex()
Am I doing a quick analysis or teaching? Use getYostIndex() Use computeYostIndex() for full control

Further Reading

  • Yost K, et al. (2001). Socioeconomic status and breast cancer incidence in California. Cancer Causes & Control, 12(8), 703–711.
  • Yang J, et al. (2014). Developing an area-based socioeconomic measure from ACS data. Cancer Prevention Research, 7(7).
  • Package source: https://github.com/HanLabCollaboration/ReproduceYostIndex
